file_name: Quaker Chemical Corporation - NON COMPETITION AND NON SOLICITATION AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Each Seller agrees that for a period commencing on the Effective Date and ending two years after the Closing Date (the "Non- Compete Period"), it shall not, other than solely through its direct or indirect ownership of Buyer's capital stock or any other interests in Buyer, directly, or indirectly, including through or on behalf of a subsidiary, anywhere in the world, excluding India: (i) own, manage, operate or control any business which competes with any Combined Business or (ii) be or become a shareholder, partner, member or owner of any Person who is engaged in any Combined Business; provided, however that nothing in this Agreement shall: (i) prohibit or restrict any Seller, directly or indirectly, from owning, as a passive investor, not more than five (5%) percent collectively and in the aggregate of any class of outstanding publicly traded securities of any Person so engaged; (ii) prohibit or restrict any Seller, directly or indirectly, from engaging in such Seller's business as conducted on the Effective Date and reasonable extensions thereof, which may include routine, day-to-day transactions with any entity, and (iii) apply to or restrict any business of which a Seller acquires control after the Effective Date provided that the acquired business did not receive more than $25,000,000 of its aggregate net sales (as measured during the 12 full calendar months prior to such acquisition) from product lines included within the definition of Company Business. Each Seller agrees that this covenant is reasonable with respect to its duration, geographical area and scope.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Seller agrees that for a period commencing on the Effective Date and ending two years after the Closing Date (the "Non- Compete Period"), it shall endeavor not to, other than solely through its direct or indirect ownership of Buyer's capital stock or any other interests in Buyer, directly, or indirectly, including through or on behalf of a subsidiary, anywhere in the world, excluding India: (i) own, manage, operate or control any business which competes with any Combined Business or (ii) be or become a shareholder, partner, member or owner of any Person who is engaged in any Combined Business; provided, however that nothing in this Agreement shall: (i) prohibit or restrict any Seller, directly or indirectly, from owning, as a passive investor, not more than five (5%) percent collectively and in the aggregate of any class of outstanding publicly traded securities of any Person so engaged; (ii) permit any Seller, directly or indirectly, to engage in such Seller's business as conducted on the Effective Date and extensions thereof, which may include routine, day-to-day transactions with any entity, and (iii) apply to or restrict any business of which a Seller acquires control after the Effective Date provided that the acquired business did not substantially exceed $25,000,000 of its aggregate net sales (as measured during the 12 full calendar months prior to such acquisition) from product lines included within the definition of Company Business. Each Seller agrees that this covenant is reasonable with respect to its duration, geographical area and scope.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall not" to "shall endeavor not to" weakens the non-compete obligation, making it less strict and enforceable. Also, changing "reasonable extensions thereof" to "extensions thereof" and "did not receive more than $25,000,000" to "did not substantially exceed $25,000,000" makes the clauses more vague, thus creating more wiggle room.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding non-compete agreements requires them to be strictly construed and reasonable.
perturbation.law_citation: Pennsylvania Uniform Trade Secrets Act (PUTSA), 12 Pa.C.S. § 5301
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania courts require non-compete agreements to be strictly construed. Using the term 'endeavor' is not strictly construable, which contradicts the intent of the law. The term extensions and substantially exceed also create unnecessary ambiguity.

perturbation.justification: The change from "shall not" to "shall endeavor not to" weakens the non-compete agreement, making it ambiguous and less enforceable. The Law Explanation states that Pennsylvania courts require non-compete agreements to be strictly construed, and using the term 'endeavor' is not strictly construable, which is contradictory. Further, the change in amount creates unnecessary ambiguity by moving from a definitive amount to an ambiguously high number by changing the language to "did not substantially exceed $25,000,000" from "did not receive more than $25,000,000". This undermines the original intent of the non-compete agreement, creating a legal contradiction under the definition for Ambiguous Legal Obligation. The provided snippets do not contain any relevant statutes to strengthen or weaken my analysis.

perturbation.original_text: Each Seller agrees that for a period commencing on the Effective Date and ending three years after the Closing Date (the "Non-Solicit Period"), each Seller shall not, directly or indirectly: (i) induce, solicit, recruit or attempt to persuade any employee of the Combined Business to terminate his or her employment with the Buyer or any of its subsidiaries, or (ii) solicit the employment of any of the employees of the Combined Business. Notwithstanding the above, Sellers shall not be restricted from (1) soliciting for employment or hiring former employees of Buyer or the Company (including their respective subsidiaries) whose employment was terminated by Buyer or the Company (including their respective subsidiaries) at least six months prior to such initial solicitation by such Seller or (2) soliciting employees of the Combined Business by means of a general solicitation through a public medium or general or mass mailing that is not specifically targeted at employees or former 3 employees of the Combined Business; provided, however, that this clause (2) shall not permit any Seller to hire any such employees during the Non-Solicit Period.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Seller will consider not to, for a period commencing on the Effective Date and ending three years after the Closing Date (the "Non-Solicit Period"), each Seller shall not, directly or indirectly: (i) induce, solicit, or attempt to persuade any employee of the Combined Business to terminate his or her employment with the Buyer or any of its subsidiaries, or (ii) solicit the employment of any of the employees of the Combined Business. Notwithstanding the above, Sellers may solicit for employment or hire former employees of Buyer or the Company (including their respective subsidiaries) whose employment was terminated by Buyer or the Company (including their respective subsidiaries).
perturbation.explanation: Changing "agrees that... shall not" to "will consider not to... shall not" introduces ambiguity. Also, removing the six-month waiting period and allowing them to solicit terminated employees removes the definite obligation. Deleting the explicit restriction on hiring employees solicited through general means weakens the protection against indirect solicitation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law requires non-solicitation agreements to be reasonable and clearly defined.
perturbation.law_explanation: The phrase 'will consider not to' weakens the obligation, making it vague and unenforceable. Removing the six-month rule regarding former employees weakens the agreements which may cause the Non-Solicit to be seen as unreasonable, and thus void under PA law. The provided articles describe PA law regarding Non-solicitation agreements. They are difficult to enforce if they are unreasonable.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the non-solicitation agreement. Changing "agrees that... shall not" to "will consider not to... shall not" creates a vague obligation, making enforcement difficult. The law explanation states this weakens the obligation, making it vague and unenforceable. The removal of the six-month rule and the deletion of restrictions on hiring employees solicited through general means further diminish the protection against solicitation. The Law Explanation also states the removal of the six-month rule weakens the agreements which may cause the Non-Solicit to be seen as unreasonable, and thus void under PA law. Thus, the change contradicts the requirements for a valid non-solicitation agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties shall execute such further instruments and take such other actions as the other party shall reasonably request in order to effectuate the purposes of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties may execute such further instruments and consider other actions as the other party may request in order to perhaps effectuate the purposes of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall execute" to "may execute", "shall reasonably request" to "may request", and "in order to effectuate" to "in order to perhaps effectuate" significantly weakens the obligation to cooperate, making it discretionary and uncertain. The introduction of 'perhaps' makes the obligation to effectuate the purposes of the agreement entirely uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law emphasizes good faith and fair dealing in contractual obligations.
perturbation.law_citation: Pennsylvania law - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts. By weakening the obligation to cooperate and adding uncertainty, the modified text suggests a potential for bad faith, conflicting with the underlying principle of good faith required in contract performance under Pennsylvania law. The case supports good faith and the modified text supports potentially acting in bad faith.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity, weakening the original obligation to cooperate. The original text used 'shall execute,' indicating a mandatory action, while the revised text uses 'may execute,' making the action discretionary. Similarly, 'shall reasonably request' became 'may request,' further reducing the obligation's strength. The addition of 'perhaps effectuate' introduces significant uncertainty. The Law Explanation states Pennsylvania law implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts. The original text ensures effectuation, but the modified text supports potentially acting in bad faith. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not helpful, the Law Explanation coupled with the changed text show contradiction to good faith requirements in contracts.
